Raw text: The problem is also connected to too high expectations. A team has to deal with those no matter what. The resulting pressure can sometimes lead to greater performance but in 9 out 10 cases it does the opposite. Look at Italy, they were so afraid of losing it again that they were barely able to move. From 2006 to 2018 every reigning champion was eliminated in the group stage. Not because they forgot how to play they simply cracked under pressure. Another thing is that underdogs always have an advantage in those cases. Extra motivation against greater teams and nothing left to lose make a toxic mix against a team that’s having a hard time with too much pressure and overly high expectations. Something that’s not exclusive to Turkey but seemingly especially rampant there is a combination of pride, defiance and entitlement. After their third place in 2002 it seems like a significant amount of Turkish fans decided that this is their rightful place in football. When they failed to qualify in the European qualifiers some blamed it on Europe generally not treating Turkey fairly and without the respect they deserve. (A sentiment that’s not totally unfounded in other aspects of the relationship between Turkey and Europe.) As corruption in Turkish football is more common than in other countries, the notion that the deck was stacked against them isn’t far fetched in the eyes of many fans. When they finally made the qualification with a young superstar player from Real Madrid and were grouped with nations deemed weaker, the expectations were extremely high. Many expected them to at least claim third place again. In reality the star isn’t ready yet to carry the team, the team isn’t really working together and they didn’t have the bit of luck that’s always necessary in football.
Label: Opinion

Raw text: Played against a decent South American team, and Australia who are amongst the top 2 or 3 in the Asian continent. Both well drilled sides and there is no shame in losing against them. Raw text: Fair point. A brand new manager with a clear philosophy is exactly what a pre-tournament model handles worst, it leans on squad quality and historic priors, not a few months of Queiroz drilling a low block. Where I'd push back slightly: the headline that England are heavy favourites comes from the talent gap, which the manager change doesn't really move. If anything Queiroz sitting deep makes the underdog outcome, Ghana nicking a draw, more likely not less, which is the avoid-defeat scenario the model already flags. So I'd treat it as a baseline for the quality gap, then adjust the tactical shape on top, basically what you did watching the Panama game.
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: I literally just explained this mate. Outcomes are as follows: USA progress regardless. If Australia and Paraguay draw that’s 4 points for each and Türkiye can only max on 3 points leaving them in 4th. Raw text: The new format has, unfortunately, very likely robbed us of a fantastic competitive finish to group D in Paraguay’s game with Australia. Now Turkey have lost they are out if the tournament regardless of their score and the score in the other game due to head to head. Even if they beat the USA 10-0 they still go home as they have lost to the other two sides. The USA are automatically winners due to the head to head as well regardless of how much the Turks can hypothetically beat them. As Australia and Paraguay are both on 3 points, it actually makes a lot of sense for them to just play for a draw. 4 points in third is guaranteed to get you through to the next round, so Paraguay may well see this as being a better option than to risk trying to get second by beating australia. Australia also get second if they draw so they have no incentive risk attacking paraguay.
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: As I understand it it goes H2H, goal difference (differential lol it gets me every time) and then discipline. So for example, in Group C, if Brazil and Morroco win their next game, whoever has the best goal difference wins the group. I do get the argument for it in that if you beat a team and finish on level points, it does feel like you should get something for that. My complaint is, aside from stuff like this eliminating teams to early, it punishes weaker sides who manage to get narrow defeats or draws (even wins sometimes) to big teams, which is often a great achievement. I suppose you can argue it incentivises the smaller teams to go hell for leather to beat each other but I don’t know how much it has actually done this

Raw text: I would say that you need Scotland to draw for sure. If they beat Brazil, they can't finish 3rd. They get 3rd with a draw or loss, but a draw puts them in better place to actually advance. Beyond that, there's still way too many results that can still play out based on which 8 teams are the top 3rd place finishers.
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: Correct. For example, if the 3rd place winners come from groups C, F, G, H, I, J, K, and L, then it would be Scotland. You could look at each of those groups and try to see what results would help that along (easiest would be how to get 3 teams with 4 or more points each). You could also look at the other groups (A, B, D, and E) to try and see what results could keep 3rd place with very few points. However, that is just one of like 495 different options. There are a number of combinations of the 8 that cause group C to play Germany, but there are also other arrangements where it could be D or F, etc. As groups become settled, some combinations will no longer be possible, but realistically there could still be dozens of outcomes still alive depending on the final group pairings.
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: Many factors: Colonialism. It's just weird how the likes of Portugal, France and even Scotland get a pass for their colonialism though. Everyone is getting all starry eyed over Norwegian fans doing their Viking rowing gimmick, acting like Vikings weren't colonists. Perceived arrogance of the English, such as with the It's Coming Home song. It's a humorous song and it's a fun way to get behind the team, but so many non-English take it seriously. Also, they think our press/population hypes up our players too much. People think we're have an exceptional hooligan culture. This is probably one of the most ridiculous ones seeing as hooligan was massively stamped out in this country and there are clearly countries that are far worse for it nowadays if you actually pay attention, but people were genuinely posting about how they were supporting Italy over England in Euro 2020 because "English fans are racist and violent". Italy has FAR surpassed England for fan violence this century, and I don't think they're any less racist than the English - in fact I'd say there are way more openly racist incidents in football in their country compared to ours, such as widespread monkey chanting which simply doesn't happen in England. Ultimately I think a lot of it comes down to the fact that people who speak English as either a first or second language are hyper aware about the bad (and good) of English culture because English is one of/the only language they speak. The same thing is happening outside of football with right wing propaganda machines convincing people that the UK is an exceptionally dangerous European country when it simply isn't
Label: Opinion

Raw text: SUI - CAN as a Canadian, but looking at the brackets...if we beat / draw with Switzerland, it seems like we'll play Mexico (should they get even 1 point or the rest of the bracket collapses) and if we lose, we'll play any one of several teams in the 2B slot vs 3rd e/f/g/i/h. Am I understanding this right?
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: You win your group if you beat/draw with Switzerland so no. That’s where you’d play the 3rd place teams you mentioned. If you lose, you’d play 2A who is expected to be South Korea but could still be Czechia or South Africa.
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: Biased take from a very biased corner: I genuinely don't think anyone wants to draw Norway in the knockouts. Yeah yeah — ranked outside the top 30, first World Cup since '98, thin defense. I know. But Haaland just finished as the TOP scorer of the entire European qualifying campaign (16 goals), with Ødegaard feeding him behind. In a one-off knockout you don't need to be the better team, you need one guy who can settle it in a single moment. Norway have arguably the most dangerous one alive. I'm not crazy enough to call them champions. But quarterfinal? I'd take that bet. And whoever draws them is NOT sleeping well the night before.
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: Canada–Switzerland is interesting. If I’m not mistaken, Switzerland beat Bosnia 4–1, while Canada only managed a 1–1 draw against Bosnia, which would suggest Switzerland has the edge. But then again, Switzerland drew 1–1 with Qatar, whereas Canada absolutely thrashed Qatar 6–0.
Label: Analytical/Predictive

Raw text: The very possibility that Brazil could end up 3rd place in the group is absolutely mind-blowing to me. This is a team that is typically DOMINANT, I can't think of a time they've advanced without at least 7 points and they usually run the table with 9. Obviously they'll still advance but still, crazy stuff.
